questions.prompt: Grammar — Put the words in the correct order: (did / when / you / arrive)
questions.explanation: A past question with an action verb is: question word + did + subject + base verb → When did you arrive? "When you did arrive" keeps statement order, "did you arrived" double-marks the past (the -ed lives on the helper did), and "When arrived you?" drops the helper did.

The error is (d): swim has one short vowel + one consonant, so the m doubles before -ing — swimming, not "swiming". The correct ones drop a silent -e before -ing: make → making, write → writing, come → coming.

questions.prompt: Vocabulary — Which word is the odd one out?
questions.explanation: Kitchen, bedroom and bathroom are all rooms inside a house, but a garden is outside, so it is the odd one out. The trap is that all four belong to a home, but only the indoor rooms form the matching set.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Comparison: "This chair is ___ comfortable ___ that one; they feel exactly the same."
questions.explanation: To say two things are equal we use as + adjective + as: as comfortable as that one. "more … than" would make one chair greater (but they're the same), "then" is a time word (not than), and "the …est" is the superlative pattern, not a comparison of two chairs.

questions.prompt:
Reading — Read: "Hana wanted to buy a present for her mother. She had a little money, just enough for some flowers, but not enough for the scarf she liked."
What could Hana buy?

options.text: Both the scarf and the flowers.

questions.explanation: The text says she had "just enough for some flowers, but not enough for the scarf", so she could buy some flowers (a). She could not afford the scarf (so b and c are out), and "a little money" still means she had some, so "nothing at all" (d) is wrong.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Future: "Our team is winning 3–0 with five minutes left. They ___ win the match!" (the evidence is clear)
questions.explanation: A prediction from clear evidence in front of you uses be going to; with they the form is are going to: They are going to win. "are going" is missing the to, "going to" is missing the be (are), and "will to" is never correct because will takes no to.
